title: Dog Red Fleshy Lump in Eye Corner: Understanding Cherry Eye
meta_desc: Discover what that red lump in your dog's eye might be. Learn about Cherry Eye, urgency levels, and how a photo can help your veterinarian.
content_html:
What is the red fleshy lump?

A red, fleshy protrusion in the corner of your dog's eye is most commonly a prolapsed gland of the third eyelid, frequently referred to as 'Cherry Eye.' Dogs have a third eyelid located in the inner corner of the eye which contains a tear-producing gland. When the connective tissue holding this gland in place weakens, the gland 'pops out,' appearing as a pink or bright red mass.

Is this an emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is Medium. While Cherry Eye is usually not a painful or life-threatening emergency in the immediate short term, it should not be ignored. If left untreated, the gland may become irritated, inflamed, or infected. Chronic exposure can also lead to decreased tear production, potentially causing long-term eye health issues. You should contact your veterinarian for an appointment within the next 24 to 48 hours.

Why a photo helps with triage

Taking a clear, close-up photo of your dog's eye is an excellent first step for triage. A visual record allows your veterinarian to assess the size, color, and exact location of the lump before you arrive at the clinic. This helps them differentiate between a simple prolapse and other conditions, such as a cyst or a tumor, and allows them to prioritize your dog's care appropriately based on the severity of the inflammation.

faqs.question: Can I push the lump back into place myself?
faqs.answer: No, you should never attempt to manually push the gland back in. This can cause significant pain, damage to the eye, or introduce infection. Only a veterinarian should handle the delicate tissue of the eye.

faqs.question: Will Cherry Eye go away on its own?
faqs.answer: It is very rare for Cherry Eye to resolve permanently without medical intervention. While the gland may occasionally slide back into place temporarily, it almost always prolapses again and usually requires surgical correction.

faqs.question: Is Cherry Eye contagious to other pets?
faqs.answer: No, Cherry Eye is a structural issue caused by a weakness in the connective tissue of the eyelid. it is not an infection and cannot be passed to other dogs or humans.
